title: Let's make something happen
instruction:
Enough talking.

Let's make the computer do something.

Hit Run.
successMessage: Nice. Your code just did something real.

title: Change the words
instruction:
Right now the computer is printing someone else's message.

Let's make it print yours.

Change "Hello, world!" to "I am learning Go!" and run the code again.
hint: Find the text inside the quotes and replace it. Keep the quotes.
successMessage: Perfect. You changed the program and got a new result. That is real coding.

title: Print your own message
instruction:
Code feels better when it becomes personal.

Change the message to anything you want. Then run it.

Examples: "My name is Sam" or "One step at a time" or anything else.
hint: Replace the text inside the quotes with your own words. The quotes must stay.

codeChecks.message: Change the message to something personal — anything other than "I am learning Go!"

successMessage: Good job. You are not just running code now — you are controlling it.

title: One program, many lines
instruction:
The computer can follow more than one instruction.

One line, then the next, then the next. Top to bottom.

Add two more fmt.Println() lines so you print 3 messages in total.
hint: Copy the existing fmt.Println("Hello") line and paste it twice below. Change the text in each one.

codeChecks.message: You need at least 3 fmt.Println() lines in your program.

successMessage: Nice. Your program now does multiple steps in order.

title: Meet main
instruction:
Every runnable Go program needs a starting point.

That starting point is `func main()`.

When your code runs, Go begins there — always.

Run the program. Then take a look at the line that says func main().
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	fmt.Println("Hello")
	fmt.Println("I am learning Go")
	fmt.Println("This is fun")
}
successMessage: Good. That block is where your program always begins running.

title: Code lives inside braces
instruction:
See those curly braces `{ }` after func main()?

They are like walls. Code inside them belongs together — it all runs as part of your program.

Add one more fmt.Println() line inside the braces and run the program.
hint: Click at the end of the existing fmt.Println line, press Enter, and type a new fmt.Println("your message") line.

codeChecks.message: Add a second fmt.Println() line inside the curly braces.

successMessage: Great. You added more code inside the program's main block.

title: Meet the print tool
instruction:
There is a line at the top that says `import "fmt"`.

That brings in a tool called fmt.

And `fmt.Println(...)` uses that tool to print text to the screen.

Think of fmt as your "show this on the screen" helper.

Add another fmt.Println() with a new message and run it.
hint: Add a second fmt.Println("something") line below the first one.

codeChecks.message: Add a second fmt.Println() line with a new message.

successMessage: Nice. You used Go's print tool again.

title: Break it, then fix it
instruction:
A big part of coding is trying things.

Sometimes you will break the code. That is normal. That is part of learning.

Run the code below. Notice the error. Then fix it so the program works.
hint: Look at the string inside Println. Something is missing at the end of it.
successMessage: Beautiful. You just did something real programmers do every day: spot a problem and fix it.

title: Build the full shape again
instruction:
By now you have seen the shape of a Go program a few times.

Let's rebuild it so it starts to feel familiar.

Inside main, add two fmt.Println() lines and run the program.
hint: The curly braces are already there. Just add your two fmt.Println("something") lines inside them.

codeChecks.message: Add two fmt.Println() lines inside the func main() block.

successMessage: Well done. The structure is starting to feel natural now.

title: One last thing before variables
instruction:
You do not need to memorize everything yet.

For now, just hold on to this:

Go programs have a shape
code inside main runs
fmt.Println() prints text
changing code changes the result

That is enough for Chapter 1.

Change the message to "I am ready for variables" and run it.
hint: Find the text inside the quotes and replace it with: I am ready for variables
successMessage:
Nice work. Next, your programs will stop just printing text and start remembering information.

That is where variables come in.
